metadata.goal: The student can partition shapes into equal parts

steps.dialogue: Divide this bar into 3 parts so that each part is the same size.

prompt.text: Partition the bar into 3 equal parts.

step.dialogue: Check the spacing. Make sure all three parts are the same size.

events.name: sequential_division_guide
events.description: Bar shows faint guide marks indicating where equal divisions should be placed

step.dialogue: [event:6949aaa239ea8031] Let's think about this together. For [vocab]thirds[/vocab], you need three [event:6949aa345a787874] [vocab]equal parts[/vocab]. Try dividing the bar so each section has the same width.

events.name: animated_equal_cuts
events.description: Animated demonstration showing two vertical cut lines appearing sequentially at equal intervals, dividing the bar into three equal sections

events.name: measure_sections_equal
events.description: Measurement lines appear below each of the three sections, confirming all sections have identical width

step.dialogue: [event:6949aae09fc895c1] This can be tricky, so let's do it together. To make [vocab]thirds[/vocab], we divide the bar into three [vocab]equal parts[/vocab]. Watch as I show you where to cut. First cut here [event:6949ab12e3990605], then here. [event:6949ab4bbaea680e] Now all three sections are [event:6949ab9f2a88041b] exactly the same size. That's how [vocab]thirds[/vocab] work - three [vocab]equal parts[/vocab] make a [vocab]whole[/vocab].

on_correct.dialogue: You divided the bar into three [vocab]equal parts[/vocab].

metadata.goal: The student can partition shapes into equal parts

steps.dialogue: Divide this bar into four [vocab]equal parts[/vocab].

prompt.text: Divide the bar into 4 equal parts.

step.dialogue: Not quite. Make four [vocab]equal parts[/vocab] across the [vocab]whole[/vocab] bar.

events.name: show_equal_division_guides
events.description: Faint vertical guide lines appear showing where four equal divisions would be placed

step.dialogue: [event:6949addfdfe697a1] Let's think about this together. Four [vocab]equal parts[/vocab] means each section needs to be exactly the [event:6949ad81b1c81219] same size. Try spacing your cuts evenly.

events.name: animate_sequential_cuts
events.description: Three vertical cut lines appear sequentially, dividing the bar into four equal sections with brief pause between each cut

events.name: measure_sections_equal
events.description: Measurement indicators appear above each of the four sections, showing they are all equal width

step.dialogue: [event:6949ad2fc8d8dc22] This can be tricky, so let's work through it together. To make four [vocab]equal parts[/vocab], we need to divide the bar into sections that are all the same size. Watch as I make three cuts that split the bar evenly. [event:6949ae725b8cb250] [delay:0.5] See how each of the four parts is exactly the same width [event:6949ad11fd069154] ? That's how [vocab]fourths[/vocab] work - four [vocab]equal parts[/vocab] make a [vocab]whole[/vocab] [event:6949b18e6709c43c] .

on_correct.dialogue: Yes. You divided the bar into four [vocab]equal parts[/vocab].

metadata.goal: The student can partition shapes into equal parts

step.dialogue: Check your spacing. Make sure all 6 parts are equal.

events.name: show_equal_division_guides
events.description: Faint vertical guide lines appear showing where four equal divisions would be placed

step.dialogue: [event:690425ddc5a85648]Let's think about this together. [event:690425a1ca6393e7]Each of the 6 parts needs to be exactly the same size. Try dividing the bar so every section has equal width.

events.name: animate_sequential_cuts
events.description: Three vertical cut lines appear sequentially, dividing the bar into four equal sections with brief pause between each cut

events.name: measure_sections_equal
events.description: Measurement indicators appear above each of the four sections, showing they are all equal width

step.dialogue: [event:690425ddc5a85648]This can be tricky, so let's work through it together. To make 6 [vocab]equal parts[/vocab], we divide the bar into 6 sections with the same width. Watch as I show you: first we split it in half, [event:6904260011c5ec14] then we divide each half into 3 equal pieces. That gives us 6 [vocab]equal parts[/vocab] total. See how each section is exactly the same size?

on_correct.dialogue: Correct. You divided the bar into 6 [vocab]equal parts[/vocab].

metadata.goal: The student can partition shapes into equal parts

steps.dialogue: Divide this rectangle bar into 8 [vocab]equal parts[/vocab].

step.dialogue: Not quite. Make 8 [vocab]equal parts[/vocab] across the [vocab]whole[/vocab] bar.

events.name: show_equal_division_guides
events.description: Faint vertical guide lines appear showing where four equal divisions would be placed

step.dialogue: [event:690426723d9d3b28]Let's think about this together. [event:6904265059b916d3]You need 8 parts that are all the same size. Try spacing your cuts evenly across the bar.

events.name: animate_sequential_cuts
events.description: Three vertical cut lines appear sequentially, dividing the bar into four equal sections with brief pause between each cut

events.name: measure_sections_equal
events.description: Measurement indicators appear above each of the four sections, showing they are all equal width

step.dialogue: [event:690426723d9d3b28]This can be tricky, so let's do it together. To make 8 [vocab]equal parts[/vocab], we need to divide the bar into 8 sections of the same size. Watch as I place 7 cuts evenly across the bar [event:690426a0f43b7fcc]- that creates 8 [vocab]equal parts[/vocab]. That's how [vocab]eighths[/vocab] work - 8 [vocab]equal parts[/vocab] make a [vocab]whole[/vocab].

on_correct.dialogue: Right! You divided the bar into 8 [vocab]equal parts[/vocab].

metadata.goal: The student can recognize and identify equal versus unequal parts

steps.dialogue: Look at these three bars. Select the bar that has parts that are all the same size.

prompt.text: Select the bar with equal parts.

step.dialogue: Check which bar has parts that are all the same size.

events.name: sections_glow_together
events.description: Bar top's equal sections glow together to show they are the same size

events.name: largest_smallest_pulse
events.description: Bar middle's largest and smallest sections pulse to show size differences

events.name: largest_smallest_pulse
events.description: Bar bottom's largest and smallest sections pulse to show size differences

step.dialogue: Let's think about this together. [event:6904276a56d9a3a0][vocab]Equal parts[/vocab] means all the pieces are exactly the same size. Look carefully at each bar's sections.

events.name: measure_sections_equal
events.description: Animated overlay showing measurement lines confirming both sections are equal width

events.name: measure_sections_unequal
events.description: Animated overlay showing measurement lines highlighting size differences between sections

events.name: measure_sections_unequal
events.description: Animated overlay showing measurement lines highlighting size differences between sections

step.dialogue: This can be tricky, so let's work through it together. The top bar has two parts [event:6904285edb250def] that are exactly the same size - that's [vocab]equal parts[/vocab]. The middle and bottom bars have parts that are different sizes [event:6904287aaeea6c08] - those are unequal. See how all parts need to be the same for [vocab]equal parts[/vocab].

on_correct.dialogue: Yes. You found the bar with [vocab]equal parts[/vocab].

metadata.goal: The student can recognize and identify equal versus unequal parts

steps.dialogue: Some of these bars are divided into [vocab]equal parts[/vocab], and some aren't. Select all the bars with [vocab]equal parts[/vocab].

prompt.text: Select all bars with equal parts.

step.dialogue: Not quite. Look carefully at which bars have parts that are all the same size.

events.name: pulse_largest_smallest

events.name: glow_equal_parts
events.description: All four equal sections of bar_3 glow together to show they match

events.name: pulse_largest_smallest
events.description: Largest and smallest sections of bar_4 pulse alternately to show size difference

step.dialogue: Let's think about this together. [event:690428cf9506829a] [vocab]Equal parts[/vocab] means all sections in a bar are exactly the same size. Check each bar to see if all its parts match.

events.name: measure_sections_equal
events.description: Animated measurement lines appear showing all four sections of bar_1 are identical width

events.name: measure_sections_unequal
events.description: Animated measurement lines appear highlighting different widths between sections of bar_2

events.name: measure_sections_equal
events.description: Animated measurement lines appear showing all four sections of bar_3 are identical width

events.name: measure_sections_unequal
events.description: Animated measurement lines appear highlighting different widths between sections of bar_4

step.dialogue: This can be tricky, so let's work through it together. For [vocab]equal parts[/vocab], every section must be the same width. [event:6904296006453f4b]Bar 1 has three sections that are all equal. Bar 2's sections are different sizes - not equal. [event:6904297f9484c98e]Bar 3 has four equal sections. Bar 4's sections are unequal. So bars 1 and 3 have [vocab]equal parts[/vocab]. That's how we identify [vocab]equal parts[/vocab] - all sections must match exactly.

on_correct.dialogue: Right. You identified both bars with [vocab]equal parts[/vocab].

metadata.goal: The student can recognize and identify equal versus unequal parts

steps.dialogue: Here's a bar divided into 5 parts. Are all the parts the same size?

step.dialogue: Not quite. Look closely at the size of each part.

events.name: pulse_smallest_and_largest
events.description: The smallest piece and largest piece of bar_1 pulse alternately to highlight the size difference

step.dialogue: Let's think about this together. Compare the smallest [event:69042a25bd66ae1b] part to the largest part. Are they the same width?

events.name: measure_sections_unequal
events.description: Animated overlay showing measurement lines on each section of bar_1, highlighting the width differences between sections

step.dialogue: This can be tricky, so let's work through it together. Watch as I measure each part. [event:690429e8d9f42bf7] See how some parts are wider than others? When parts have different sizes, they're unequal. That's why the answer is 'no' - these parts are not all the same size.

on_correct.dialogue: You identified that the parts are unequal.

metadata.goal: The student can recognize and identify equal versus unequal parts

step.dialogue: This can be tricky, so let's work through it together. If you look at the top and the middle bars, you can see that the parts are not the [event:6949b3a07a4737d1] same size. But the bottom bar has 6 equal parts. That's how [vocab]equal parts[/vocab] work - [event:69042a7c8e57169f] every section must be exactly the same size.

on_correct.dialogue: Correct. You identified the bar with [vocab]equal parts[/vocab].

metadata.goal: The student can shade one part to represent a unit fraction

steps.dialogue: Which of these bars shows a third shaded?

prompt.text: Select the bar showing one-third shaded.

step.dialogue: Check how many parts are shaded in each bar.

events.name: pulse_shaded_sections
events.description: The two shaded sections in the top bar pulse to show there are 2 parts shaded

events.name: pulse_shaded_sections
events.description: The one shaded section in the middle bar pulses to show there is 1 part shaded

events.name: pulse_shaded_sections
events.description: The one shaded section in the bottom bar pulses to show there is 1 part shaded

step.dialogue: Let's think about this together. [fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]One-third[/fraction] means 1 part shaded out of 3 [vocab]equal parts[/vocab]. Look at how many [event:69042b711256829b] parts are shaded in each bar.

events.name: count_shaded_parts
events.description: Animated counting overlay shows '2 shaded out of 3' with numbers appearing over shaded sections

events.name: count_shaded_parts
events.description: Animated counting overlay shows '1 shaded out of 3' with number appearing over shaded section, then checkmark appears

events.name: count_shaded_parts
events.description: Animated counting overlay shows '1 shaded out of 2' with numbers appearing over sections

step.dialogue: This can be challenging, so let's work through it together. [fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]One-third[/fraction] means 1 part out of 3 [vocab]equal parts[/vocab]. The top bar has 2 parts shaded, [event:69042c56fcc8e60b] so that's [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]two-thirds[/fraction]. The bottom bar only has 2 parts total, [event:69042cbe8acb0ef7] so that's [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]. The middle bar has 3 [event:69042cd2f8508105] [vocab]equal parts[/vocab] with exactly 1 [event:6949b56f335740d2] shaded. That's how [fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]one-third[/fraction] works - 1 out of 3 [vocab]equal parts[/vocab].

on_correct.dialogue: You found the bar with one-third shaded.

metadata.goal: The student can identify and maintain the whole when working with fractions

steps.dialogue: This bar has one part shaded. How many [vocab]equal parts[/vocab] are there in the [vocab]whole[/vocab] bar?

prompt.text: How many equal parts does the whole bar have?

step.dialogue: Count all the parts in the [vocab]whole[/vocab] bar, both shaded and unshaded.

events.name: pulse_all_sections
events.description: Both sections of the bar pulse sequentially to emphasize counting all parts

step.dialogue: Let's think about this together. [event:69042d2934e8f9c7]The [vocab]whole[/vocab] bar includes both the shaded part and the unshaded part. Count them both.

events.name: overlay_counting
events.description: Animated counting overlay appears, numbering each section sequentially (1, 2) with emphasis on both shaded and unshaded parts

step.dialogue: This can be tricky, so let's work through it together. The [vocab]whole[/vocab] bar has two [vocab]equal parts[/vocab] - [event:69042d2934e8f9c7] one shaded part and one unshaded part. We count both parts to find the total. That's 2 [vocab]equal parts[/vocab] in the [vocab]whole[/vocab] bar.

on_correct.dialogue: Yes. You counted all the parts in the [vocab]whole[/vocab] bar.

metadata.goal: The student can identify and maintain the whole when working with fractions

Even though two parts are shaded, we count all the parts to find the [vocab]whole[/vocab]. That's how [vocab]thirds[/vocab] work - 3 [vocab]equal parts[/vocab] make a [vocab]whole[/vocab].

on_correct.dialogue: Right. You counted all the parts in the [vocab]whole[/vocab] bar.

step.dialogue: This can be tricky, so let's work through it together. The [vocab]whole[/vocab] bar has four [event:69042e2a0f0eab9c] [vocab]equal parts[/vocab] total. Let me show you: one, two, three, four. We count all the parts - both the shaded ones and the unshaded one. That's how we know the [vocab]whole[/vocab] has four [vocab]equal parts[/vocab].

on_correct.dialogue: Correct. You counted all the parts in the [vocab]whole[/vocab] bar.

Let me count them with you: [event:69042e6e0a4f915e] one, two, three, four, five [vocab]equal parts[/vocab] total. step.dialogue: This is tricky, so let's work through it together. The [vocab]whole[/vocab] bar has all the parts - watch as we count: [event:69042ebb1e00f8cf] one, two, three, four, five, six. Even though only four are shaded, the [vocab]whole[/vocab] bar is made of six [vocab]equal parts[/vocab]. That's how fractions work - we always count all parts in the [vocab]whole[/vocab].

on_correct.dialogue: You counted all the parts in the [vocab]whole[/vocab] bar.

step.dialogue: Let's think about this together. [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]One-half[/fraction] [event:69042f9ad8152155]means the [vocab]whole[/vocab] is divided into two [vocab]equal parts[/vocab]. Check which bar has parts that are exactly the same size.

step.dialogue: This can be tricky, so let's work through it together. For [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one-half[/fraction], we need exactly two [vocab]equal parts[/vocab]. The top bar shows two parts [event:69042fb8c983d4b0] that are the same size - that's [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one-half[/fraction]. The middle bar has two parts,[event:69042fe137fd4401] but they're different sizes, so it's not [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one-half[/fraction]. The bottom bar has three [event:6904300528b569b3] parts, not two. That's how [vocab]halves[/vocab] work - two [vocab]equal parts[/vocab] make [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one-half[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: Yes. You identified the bar with [vocab]one-half[/vocab].

step.dialogue: This can be tricky, so let's work through it together. For a fraction to be accurate, all parts must be equal. [event:6904305f35987c82]The top bar has three parts, but they're different sizes - see how some are bigger than others? The bottom bar has three [vocab]equal parts[/vocab], so one shaded section is truly [fraction numerator=1 denominator=3]one-third[/fraction]. That's how [vocab]thirds[/vocab] work - three [vocab]equal parts[/vocab] make a [vocab]whole[/vocab].

options: Yes, it is one of six equal parts.

step.dialogue: Let's think about this together. For a fraction to work, what must be true about all the parts? Look at the sections in this bar.

step.dialogue: This is tricky, so let's work through it together. For any fraction to be valid, all the parts must be exactly the same size. Look at this bar - [event:690430e5cc61ce19] some sections are wider than others. Since the parts aren't [vocab]equal parts[/vocab], we can't call the shaded part [fraction numerator=1 denominator=6]one-sixth[/fraction]. That's why fractions always need [vocab]equal parts[/vocab].

on_correct.dialogue: Correct. You identified that fractions need [vocab]equal parts[/vocab].
